textPlain: Also stamp-scented croissants?

Unfortunately, WebSmell-o-Vision has not yet been unilaterally pushed by the dudes at Google to do this news justice, but as a croissant connaisseur I would have at least appreciated a webp of the thing instead of some random archive image. It’s a stamp, for christ’s sake.There is a pic here and it’s quite nice imo: https://www.wopa-plus.com/en/stamps/product/&pid=105515

Wonderful, I want one!  Say what you will, the French have good taste.Naturally, this also poses some serious questions:- Will this lead to more croissants being eaten?  Everyone within reach of such a stamp could be affected.  Will bakeries have to brace themselves?- How will this affect the stamp collector scene?  Will they flip upside down to get one of these?  Will they eat more croissants?Only time can tell!

I'm quite sad that here in Belgium companies now often use stamps that aren't actual little bits of paper anymore but ink stamps on the envelope.I hope that doesn't spread. I have a generational stamp collection that I'd love to keep expanding.
Does the IRS use stamps too?
Who said Europe can't innovate
Do they taste buttery when you lick them?

I wish we had croussants here in teh states (tiny speciality shops not withstanding). They are a rare. instead we get a weird butter flavored croissant shaped bread with nothing of the texture or aroma of the real thing.

Nice, we got 4 fruits in Switzerland:
https://shop.post.ch/en/stamps-philately/philately/single-st...
Or is it that they made all croissants stamp-scented instead?
Has science gone too far?
La Frenchtech at its peak.
Can't wait for the cheeses
> artisan croissants [...] “emblem of French gastronomy,”and yet italian cornetti are better grin
I want one!  Seriously, this should be a thing with more scents.
I am holding off for the red wine flavoured one.
How are they going to discourage roaches from eating your mail?
Now this is the future I’ve been waiting for
La Poste might have invented the first non-vegan stamps in history.I hope they don't turn rancid.
Try and find a good one in France. Harder than you’d think

Better than the time that a utility company mailed out a gas-scented flyer to teach people what a gas leak smelled like. 911 was inundated with gas leak calls.(Search is so fucking useless these days that I can't find anything about it, but pretty sure it was in New York.)
Has there ever been any sort of even-remotely-successful smell-o-vision? We can recreate input for many of our other senses - music, visuals, touch - so why not smell?
Friendly counterpoint (I know, not actually responsive to your comment, but tangentially related): https://www.science.org/doi/10.1126/science.ade4401
I'm holding out for a <smell> HTML tag.
But wopa-plus does even mention the essential detail?https://www.laposte.fr/pp/c/timbre-croissant-au-beurre
Here I was misreading the article that it was croissant-shaped, not scented, until I saw a picture.
I can almost smell breakfast…
Who said we can't have nice things?
I maintain that croissant's are just sticks of butter someone accidentally spilled flour on.

It's not just the states, pretty much all of Europe and even in France you'll find cheap croissants made with margarine. You always need to go to a real small bakery (tiny specialty shop as you say) to find real, freshly baked croissants. That's just a bit easier in France. The problem isn't where you live, it's where you shop.
Same with bagels here in the UK. Round bread with a hole in it, for the most part.

Costco will sell you an implausibly large number of croissants. They're pretty good if you eat them the same day. They go bad very rapidly, which makes sense given that the minimum purchase size is something like 24.They're still better than Costco's "bagels", which do not share any properties of bagels other than the toroidal shape.
Because most in North America are made with margarine or a mix of margarine and butter, so much of the butter "taste" is fake.  That's also why the lamination is worse.
I miss my concrete stamps... A bit rough on the tongue but still lovely
not before the RATP series!
Given the normal process for making glue I wouldn't be so certain.
> Try and find a good one in France. Harder than you’d thinkIn Paris? Sure. You're competing with tourists. Almost any rural bakery? No. You'd have to try to find something shitty.

I remember a scratch-and-sniff version, rather than just scented (in western CT, in the 1970s - they must have bulk sent them, noone in the neighborhood actually had gas service so it wasn't in with gas bills.)  That's enough to find https://hackaday.com/2024/12/03/scratch-and-sniff-stickers-a...  and the search https://www.google.com/search?q=scratch-and-sniff+mercaptan&... actually turns up a bunch of present-day gas vendors that will send them to you on request!
Natural gas is odourless, so the smell of a gas leak is artificially chosen. They could have just made all the piped gas smell like croissants instead.
